This one had the Tupperware Party. Next one, this one had the Stanley Home Products Party. Next week, this one had the Jewelry Party. This one, et cetera, and everybody was obligated to go. In part, because they were gonna host one sometime soon, and if they didn't go, then that person wouldn't come. So this was an accepted part of Americana for almost two decades. It went on all the time in every supper. It's not that way today, of course, but it was. So if you can make this an accepted thing, whatever your thing is within a population, that's a powerful, powerful piece of leverage. 30, I'm on your page 48 with the next little list. Threshold resistance. Generally credited to a real estate developer, mall developer, died not too long ago, his name's Arnold Topman. Sydney Barrows, it's in the book UNI co-authored. Sydney interviewed Arnold. There is an Arnold Topman book titled Threshold Resistance. It's really for retailers, but it exists in everything. So threshold resistance is about how high is the threshold we are asking somebody to step over to move toward us. And most of us mess it up because we think we're not scary. So whatever it is that you do, you do it all the time. And you long ago, it lost any of its novelty to you. And you think it's as mundane and ordinary as it can possibly be, right? So like the Larry's business, the financial advisors who go, your business, financial advisors who go, your good example, who go and do the free workshop. You know, I always joke, it's for old people and you stand at the door and you wave a stake and in they come, you know. Who do these things, right? They're always somewhat flummoxed that they don't book almost everybody for an appointment. Right, it's mysterious. Because think about it, somebody had to answer an ad of some kind, they had to register, they had to get in their car, and yeah, a few of them come for the steak and the chicken, but not that many. Because think about how painful this is. So they had to get in their car, they had to go to a hotel or a country club, and they probably don't go there all the time and they don't know where it is, so they had to find it and that's uncomfortable. And then they had to come in and they had to sit in a room with strangers, with a stranger, and put themselves in a uncertain environment where God knows what is gonna happen to me. They had to do all that and then they don't book an appointment to come in, and the person just did their best dog and pony show too. So spectacular. So like your numbers are pretty good, I don't remember, but when you do a workshop, what do you book for appointments? Okay, about 60%. But now that means four out of every 10, right? Who already stepped over all these other thresholds to be in the room, four out of every 10. Don't book an appointment. Let's say one of them came for the steak. That's three out of every 10. How can this be? Well, mostly, because we don't thoroughly understand how high that last threshold is. So okay, now it's time for you and your wife to go home, get all your financial records and tax returns in a big box. I don't even know where that shit is, guy says to himself, oh, here's a list of shit you gotta get, and you gotta come in here, and you gotta strip naked financially in front of me, and I'm gonna do a financial proctology exam, and I'm gonna find a bunch of embarrassing stuff that you have been screwing up for years. I'm gonna embarrass you in front of your spouse, because I'm gonna have to talk about all the stuff I'm finding that you're a moron about. Guy says, I don't think I wanna do that, and we can't blame them, right? So it's a real high threshold, and some people put them all the way at the beginning of a process, big threshold, right? They leap them, the miracle there, see, they never got it. I said, you're asking somebody to go from a newspaper ad to coming in for three hours of exams, they gotta bring a spouse with them, they're gonna take 12 tests, they don't know exactly what these tests are, they don't know what, if you get a stick something in them, or you've got, what are you gonna do? This is a big threshold for some percentage of people. You've all seen, most people stop using them, I still have clients who use them, the free recorded message, right? Here's a free recorded message you can call, no human will answer, you don't have to talk to a human, whatever we're really doing. We're lowering the threshold for somebody to respond, because if we just give them a phone number, Harry says to himself, it's gonna be answered by a human, the human is gonna try and get me to do something I don't wanna do, they're gonna come right through the wire, up the other end of the phone and grab me, that's what's gonna happen, I'm not calling. By the way, you've all done it, you've all almost responded to something, and then backed yourself off, because essentially the threshold you were being asked to get over was too high. So when that's the case, if we lower, if we manipulate the threshold resistance, we can raise the response. Now, sometimes people will say, I don't wanna do that, because I'll trade down response, and you might. But most of the time, overall, it's a win. So here is, I'm gonna show you a couple examples. We'll stay with the financial first. So this is a piece out of a great, the only thing we wanna look at is right here, this is not in your manual, by the way, should have put a blue star, forgot, there's a blue star right there. So everyone attending this special meeting will get a copy of my new special report, sounding the alarm, monumental historical events in 2013 to 2015, the main danger retirees healthcare, retirement income and financial security is never before. If for some reason you can't join us for my meeting, at least let me send you this provocative report for a free copy of the report, call the office, there's no cost or there's no obligation, there's a website you can go to, I couldn't get her to test the free recorder message, but if I was doing it, I would have driven it to a free recorder message to get the free report. They insisted on taking a call live so they could try and flip them to the meeting because there's this profound belief that if we do this, somebody who would have come to the dinner now doesn't, and they do this instead because it's a lower threshold. And that is actually true. It will reduce, in this case, the number that come to the dinner by about 15%. Give or take. However, it'll also create a whole nother list of leads that you wouldn't have got otherwise. And if you know what to do with leads, it's the win. So you don't necessarily have to lower the threshold if you just wanna offer optional thresholds. You wanna jump, here's a high one, jump. He's gotta, you know, he's gotta take off his slippers. We gotta get dressed. We gotta make sure there's gas in the car. It's a big to do for people. You don't think it is, you go places. I don't know if I want it. I mean, it's a big, it's a big high threshold. Now we gotta go to the Holiday Inn. I don't know where the Holiday Inn is. What if it's dark? I don't drive well in the dark, right? What if we can't find our car when we come out? What if we get mugged? What if terrorists descend on the place and blow it up? I mean, you know, I don't know. Then what's gonna happen to us if we go? We're gonna be in this room. We don't know this guy. We're not gonna know anybody. We're gonna be made to answer a question and be embarrassed. I don't like that. I mean, so this threshold's real high, real high. Doesn't feel high to you. Hey, you go to seven hours all the time. You go places. You got something to put on beside yoga pants. I mean, you know, it's not a big to do for you, but it's a big to do for most people. And we greatly underestimate where the threshold is in everything we ask people to do. Therefore, in almost every case, there's a bunch of almost persuaded. In every case and at every step, there's people who almost did it, whatever it is you were asking them to do. Almost, and there's a lot of them. And so when you figure out ways to give them options that they can move slower and more timidly toward you, you start to scoop them up. Now that's no good if you don't know what to do with leads. Or you won't do anything with leads. Either way, it's no good. So if you're a car dealer and you absolutely will not do anything with leads, then none of this is any good. Then everybody's in the eat what we can kill business and we're only gonna kill what we can and we gotta do it today. But if you understand what we do with leads, then this kind of lower the threshold, get some more people to step to you a little, give them another threshold and another threshold, this thing works. Arguably the flip of this, and yet they can work together, is aggressive intrusion. Aggressive and persistent intrusion. And so the forcing your way in, the showing up often, aggressively. See, all it requires is somebody's permission. That's all it requires. And then the whole dynamic changes. So if I have said to you, yeah, I wanna hear more about hearing aids, you've now opened the door. I can show up a lot and I can be fairly aggressive. But until I have said that, then all of that aggressiveness works against you. So the most aggressive intrusion there is, usually is a knock on the door, right? That's the most aggressive thing that there is, is a knock on the door. Now we don't have a lot of door to door sales people anymore. They're out there, but they're certainly a dying breed. There was a day when America had armies of them. The dinner hour was consistently interrupted by some sales guy at the door. Day time, when mom was home, there was an army of them out there. And often they were appointment center guys. So the encyclopedia sales force was out there all day long, knocking on doors. And then getting you to agree to let them come back at five o'clock when the husband was gonna be home and we're all gonna sit down and talk about the encyclopedia. This was like armies of them, armies, untold numbers. I don't know what they're all doing. I don't know, I have no idea. But I mean, there was a lot of them. The fire alarm business alone, fire alarm business alone at its peak, home fire alarms, had 50 different companies, each one on average with 10,000 reps, running appointments, sound fire alarms. That's a lot of people. And it's pretty much gone. It exists, the shadow of a tiny shadow of its former self. But that was incredibly aggressive intrusion. So somehow there has to be a threshold for somebody crosses that makes it now accepted. So there has to be a need, there has to be a desire. So the fire alarm guys all moved the crews to wherever there had been a fire. So the minute there's a fire, it's in the community newspaper or it's in your neighborhood. So we just had a house burned down last year in our neighborhood. And immediately, starting with Carl. What do you think? We have a good enough fire alarm system in the house. Now this conversation never occurred. We've been there for 12, 13, 14 years. Never once has fire alarms come up. Never, never. House burned down. So if you were a fire alarm guy and you got to that neighborhood and you knocked on a door, your aggressiveness would be much differently received than if there had not been a fire. The bird guys, some of you know this because I've told it before, but it's instructive. So the bird alarm guys, which worked in crews just like the fire guys. So the crews come into a community, they all go around during the day, dating back to when mom was home, and then they set appointments to come back at night. Once it got to the point, mom wasn't home during the day, they all just descended at like four o'clock and they worked until nine o'clock at night, banging on doors to get in and pitch you on fire alarms, or burglar alarms. Now you buy a $9 ring thing, which you've seen advertised on TV and you don't need a burglar alarm. This is the dumbest product. Have you else seen this? This ring thing? Oh yeah, it goes, so it's your doorbell. It replaces your doorbell. And when it rings, it comes to your smartphone. And so you can pretend that you're home. I'm not gonna ring it. I'm gonna bang on the door. Nobody comes to the door, we're going in, right? They just made this job so much easier, it isn't even funny, right? I suspect it's a company, it's a whole team of burglars who are selling these things. They certainly ought to be selling the leads, right? I mean, so in a bad old days, the burg guys, they target your neighborhood. For two weeks before they're coming in, they go in in the dark at night and all around everybody's houses in the bushes, they leave empty liquor bottles, condoms, and girly magazines. And that's what they do in a neighborhood for about two weeks. Then the sales guys come in from the burglar arms. When they knock on a door, they are received very differently than if for two weeks there hadn't been girly magazines and liquor bottles and condoms in the bushes. And every burglar alarm company did. Standard operating procedure. Now, I don't suggest the tactic, but I suggest the strategy. You wanna think about some way that makes aggressiveness okay, that sets up a situation whereby you can do aggressive selling, aggressive follow up on leads, and they really are okay with you being that aggressive. I will make the point for the benefit of the annoying little Indian wherever he is. There you are. As I said about the car deal, that leads are no good if you don't have a system in place to persistently and completely follow up on leads. If you don't have one, that's what they do. Aggressive, mouse trapping. So let's talk about mouse trapping, number 32. So Bob Stupak, if you don't know Bob Google him, there is a book and it's a good book. I forget, but if you Google Stupak, there's a biography on it. So Stupak, if you don't know, Bob built Vegas World, the last hotel on, it's not the stratosphere in Las Vegas. So if you've been to Las Vegas recently, there's a thing right at the end of the straight, right before you go into the bad part of town, that's called the stratosphere. It began as a one floor slot machine parlor called Vegas World. And, but so this is a Bob Stupak quote. He says, when we put 50 slot machines in, I always consider that there are 50 more mouse traps. And it is our duty to extract as much money as we can from our customers. So let me tell you how mouse trappy this is. And Dustin, I promise to give this back to you, you can have it in Bob when we break, okay? I've copied the pages that I wanted. So this is 441 pages. I think it's 11 point type. It's called Addiction by Design, Machine Gambling in Las Vegas. 440 pages of how the machines are engineered to be addicted. This is how much depth. I have a book, I loaned it to the guys at Northfield when hard rock came in and I haven't got a bag. It's twice this thick, hard bound and this big, just on casino floor layout. Casino floor management maybe. But my point is the depth. The depth of this one thing about extracting money. I mean, you know a lot about both slot machine design and addiction, which is why it's worth reading by the way, because we all wanna create some kind of addiction. The depth that they go to, that micro detail of things that make a difference where the color is, where the light falls on the machine. Should it be tilted this way? Should it be tilted that way? That's all the floor layout stuff. This is all what they've done to keep you playing for one more quarter, one more nickel. They're completely confused by the way, the money. Because the penny machine is now a $3 bet. I mean, it's they've completely, people think they're playing a penny machine. I'm going down playing a penny machine. You're paying $3 every time you spend the deal. You're playing a $3 machine. I'm not playing a penny machine. So again, we'll go over, I'll say I'm gonna go play in the high rollers room. Why don't you come in and play a machine with me? I'll call it a ha. I would never play a $5 machine. I'm gonna go play the penny machine. I'm going, you're playing a $5 machine. It just says penny on it. I mean, who's kidding who? But they are kidding them. So the depth that these people go to in knowledge, in sophistication of how to move people from point A to point B, take their money, have them like it. Hardly any of us go that deep in what it is that we do. Right? I mean, we just aren't that good of a student about the two or three things that are really significant and important in our businesses. So, I'm on your page 50. So, I found you an old Al Goldstein quote. You can Google him. Al was an interesting guy. So, so I have a confession. None of you will repeat this outside of this room. So, I have a formula. That works with Carla perfectly. And she don't know it. So, this is just between you and me. No, she lost interest in what I do quite some time ago. In the previous marriage, she lost interest in what I do. The rest of the family never had any interest. So, you know, it's something. But, so no, she never, no, no, no. If you guys don't tell her, I'm safe. So, here's the formula. The question is, where would you like to go to dinner? That's the question. So, let me give you all the correct answer to this question. It has three parts. So, the first part, it's a magic phrase because it drops everybody's defenses. First part is, I don't care. You wanna write this down, it's not in your manual. It works. I don't care. The second phrase is even more powerful. You decide. Big, big. Now, here's the third part. Either Hyde Park or Outback is okay with me. You pick. Now, she thinks she's picking. She's happy because she got to pick. But I only gave her two choices. This is like kindergarten forced choice. However, this works, by the way, in a lot of other settings with a lot of people. See, it's not the reality ever. It's how somebody feels about it. It's how somebody feels about the reality that they're in. And so, people often like to feel in control. Truth of the matter is, they don't really even wanna be in control. They just like to feel like they're in control. So, here's another version of it. So, when we go to Disney, because we're Disney Vacation Club members, you gotta book everything six months ahead of time to do it. Carla manages all that, she manages it brilliant. But every time, we have the same conversation. And understand, Carla's a person who likes to go different places and try different restaurants and all of that. I don't like any of that. I got like three places I like to go. I wanna go to those three places. I don't get there enough. I don't need to experiment. But so, the question is always now, I'm gonna go book the restaurants, cause you gotta book all this shit ahead of time. What restaurants do you want me to book? Now, if I answer that question.
